mnemonics.title: XP Core Values
mnemonics.content: Remember the four core values using: 'CSCF' - Communication, Simplicity, Courage, Feedback (think: Crew Saves Captain Frank)

mnemonics.title: TDD Cycle
mnemonics.content: TDD cycle: Red (Write failing test) → Green (Make it pass) → Refactor (Improve). Like traffic: Red stop (test fails), Green go (test passes), Yellow slow down (refactor)

mnemonics.title: 12 XP Practices
mnemonics.content: 12 Practices: PP-PT (Pair Programming, Planning Game), TD-CI (Test-Driven Dev, Continuous Integration), RF-SD (Refactoring, Simple Design), CO-CS (Collective Ownership, Coding Standards), M-SP (Metaphor, Sustainable Pace), UC-OC (User Stories, On-site Customer)

mnemonics.title: XP Roles
mnemonics.content: Roles: PCT - Programmer (writes code), Customer (defines what), Tester (verifies), Coach (guides the team)

acronyms.usage: An agile software development methodology focusing on high-quality software and team productivity
A development technique where tests are written before code following Red-Green-Refactor cycle
Frequently merging code changes and running automated builds and tests
A principle suggesting not to build features until they are actually needed
A design tool used in XP for object-oriented design sessions

visualTips.visualization: Picture a square with four corners: Communication (top-left), Simplicity (top-right), Feedback (bottom-left), Courage (bottom-right) - all equally important like the corners supporting a structure
Think of a traffic light: Red = test fails (stop, think), Green = test passes (go, code works), Yellow = refactor (slow down, improve)
Imagine two people sharing one steering wheel - Driver controls (steers), Navigator watches the road (reviews) and gives directions
Think of an index card with three parts: Who (role), What (feature), Why (benefit) - written as 'As a [who], I want [what] so that [why]'

analogies.analogy: Like learning to drive with an instructor - the driver (programmer) practices while the navigator (instructor) watches for mistakes and guides
Like a relay race where runners frequently pass the baton rather than one person running the entire distance - smaller, frequent integrations are smoother
Like reorganizing a cluttered room - you're not throwing things away, just arranging them better to find things more easily
Like writing a wish list instead of a technical specification - focusing on what you want, not how to build it
Like explaining a new app to your grandmother using everyday examples rather than technical jargon
Like a marathon runner pacing themselves rather than sprinting - consistent moderate effort beats burnout
